text: Narrative 1 - 'During out loading of cargo in ZZZ; ramp crew added 24 pounds of cargo too the aft compartment. We were only carrying 7 bags total up front. We asked the ground crew via the intercom to confirm that the cargo was in the aft compartment and not in the front and they confirmed. We however never inquired on what the cargo was. Cargo load report via acars did not mention any HAZMAT was being carried. When we arrived into Boston; I conducted the walk around. The ground personal had already removed the cargo out of the aft compartment and left it on the ground. Seeing the boxes I went over to inspect too see what it was. 1 box was 'dash'. I know dash cargo we can put in either cargo compartment. Hazmat 1 can be carried but only in the forward compartment for the dry ice. Hazmat 2 is not permitted to be carried at all on our aircraft. Had we received a NOTAC in the CLR we would of catches this HAZMAT in ZZZ and had it removed.'
rationale: Ground crew loaded 24 pounds of undeclared HAZMAT cargo into the aft compartment without including it on the ACARS cargo load report, and the flight crew verified only the compartment location—not the nature of the cargo—before departure. The flight crew's own NOTAC-based screening process would have caught the HAZMAT classification had it been correctly declared on the load documentation. The root cause is a human failure by the ground personnel who tendered and loaded undeclared dangerous goods without completing the mandatory HAZMAT declaration and notification process.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'During the power-up operation at ZZZ; we encountered a communication issue with the ground personnel. Firstly; they didn't brief us on their actions; and there were no headsets or hand signals from the guide-man after the door closed.After finishing the pre-start checklist; I signaled to the ground personnel that we were ready to depart; indicated brakes on; and signaled readiness to start the engines. The person at the nose signaled clearance to start the engines. However; after starting two engines and completing the after-start flow; when I signaled with the taxi lights that we were ready to taxi; the guide-man's hands weren't in the X-stop position. As we started moving the plane for seconds; the ground personnel didn't follow the correct hand guidance from the FOM and tried to get our attention to stop. It was then that we realized another person was removing the nose gear chocks; while two engines were already running; the lack of communication put in an unsafe situation the operation in the ramp.Lack of ground personnel communication with the flight crew members. More training in order to improve the operation in an airport with an uncontrolled ramp; no gate. Ground personnel must brief the crew before close doors.'
Narrative 2 - 'As we approached the aircraft I noticed that ZZZ has no tug and the way the aircraft was positioned it looked like we would be performing a power out from the gate. In my few experiences with power out operations the station does not have an interphone to communicate with the flight crew but uses hand signals so that is what I assumed would happen in this case again. Whether ZZZ uses an interphone I am not sure; as we did not attempt to reach the ground crew via headsets. If they do not; we were not briefed as to the operations at ZZZ by the guide person. Regardless; after door closure and performing the Before Start Checklist" the Captain signaled to the guide person; requesting to start Engine 1 and was given permission. We subsequently asked and received permission to start Engine 2. After starting engine 2 we preformed the after start flow and the Captain flashed the taxi light to indicate we were ready to taxi. We then received from the guide person the "Chocks removed" signal and he was waiting for acknowledgment. We then began to roll forward and were quickly given the "stop" signal. We stopped the aircraft. The Captain said that he saw someone run out from under the nose of the aircraft. After about 5 seconds we were guided out by the guide person. We never received a salute and when we landed in ZZZ1 the Crew Chief informed me when I went down for the post flight that the GPU door was open when we parked.Regardless of whether or not ZZZ had interphones we should have discussed with the ground crew the expected procedure for the power out operation which; for us pilots; is essentially a non-normal situation.Suggestion: Review power out operations in AOM/FOM"'
rationale: Ground personnel at an uncontrolled ramp station failed to brief the flight crew on the power-out procedure before door closure, did not use headsets or standard hand signals consistently, and allowed a second crew member to remove nose gear chocks while two engines were already running—creating an unsafe sequence that the crew had no way to anticipate. Both pilots acknowledge they also did not proactively coordinate with the ground crew before starting, but the fundamental breakdown was the ground personnel's failure to execute the standard pre-departure brief and signal protocol. This is a human factors incident driven by a communication breakdown between non-aviation ramp personnel and the flight crew in a non-standard power-out environment.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Pushed from [gate] and was told to contact ground at spot 5. Contacted ground and they said to taxi Alpha-Echo-Bravo to runway 4 and we were number 1 for departure; Once on Bravo taxiway; Ground told us to monitor Tower. Once on Tower Freq; the Captain taxied as advised by Ground instructions (crossing the ILS critical area). Tower then instructed us to stop before the critical area as we crossed the ILS Critical Area. I advised Tower that we were stopping at that point. No point did the ground controller tell us to stop before the critical area. Should be noted that today both times in LGA the ILS critical area was included in the ATIS. One time ground was advising to hold short during taxi instructions; the other time they weren't saying to hold short as the ATIS had mentioned and aircraft were crossing the ILS critical area with no issues.Due diligence should always be maintained both on the ground and in the air. Safety has been and always be my first focus while operating an aircraft. Missing the assignment of stopping short of the ILS critical area should have been a priority and questioned if there was doubt. The confusion is also compounded due to the fact before this flight I have heard ground stating to hold short of the ILS critical area. We operated to ZZZ and then returned to LGA. When we returned to LGA it was overheard the controllers to hold short of the ILS critical area. The following day the same message was in the ATIS and all aircraft in front of us were not stopping short of the area.  This is not said to be an excuse; but to point out when there is factors outside the flight deck that can start a crew down the wrong path of operation of the aircraft I should take even more precautions as a First Officer.'
rationale: Ground Control issued taxi instructions routing the crew across the ILS critical area without including a hold-short instruction, despite the ATIS explicitly noting the ILS critical area—and the reporter observes that this same omission occurred inconsistently on the same day, with other crews crossing the area unchallenged. The crew followed the explicit taxi instruction as given; the hazard arose because the ground controller's clearance did not include the mandatory hold-short restriction that the ATIS had placed in effect. The procedure error lies with Ground Control's inconsistent application of an ATIS-notified restriction that should have been uniformly included in every taxi clearance affecting the ILS critical area.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I approached to land at ZZZ on Runway XX upon reaching a hover I sidestepped left to hover taxi over the taxiway. There was an old crop-dusting plane being fueled at the pump. I passed the plane approximately 80 ft. to the south of the plane over the taxiway hovering between 4 and 8 ft. I proceeded to land next to the ambulance waiting at the ramp. As we passed the plane one of my crew members stated that the small plane began to turn on its own. I landed at the H and shut down. After I shut down; I spoke with the pilot of the plane. I told him that I was sorry for his inconvenience. I told him that I gave him plenty of space when I hover taxied past him. I asked does your plane have a parking brake - he replied no; it is not equipped with one. The plane also did not have wheel chocks in place. I took pictures of the plane and called Person A. I gave the man Person A's contact information and my own. In my professional opinion as a dual rated pilot; the damage to this man's plane occurred purely out of his own negligence; one for not having a parking brake and two not having his wheels chocked. His plane would not have been affected by my rotor wash; if he had secured his plane properly. Have all airplane pilots that are fueling their aircraft with no brakes; to place chocks on the wheels.'
rationale: A parked crop-duster aircraft had no parking brake and no wheel chocks in place while being fueled at a ramp location where other aircraft operations were occurring, causing rotor wash from a passing helicopter to displace it. The helicopter pilot operated at a distance he judged professionally adequate, and the displacement would not have occurred had the fixed-wing aircraft been secured per basic ramp safety practice. This is a human factors incident in the 'Other' category where the hazard was created by the fixed-wing pilot's failure to properly secure the aircraft before fueling—a fundamental ramp safety omission by a non-ATC operator.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I was repositioning the Super Cub to a family owned farm due to ramp construction at the ZZZ where the aircraft is normally based. I have landed on the farm several times in various aircraft and had walked the landing location. After several low reconnaissance passes and noting tall weeds; I selected a landing location that had no obvious tall weeds. After making an uneventful landing; during the roll-out the main tires hit some dense weed undergrowth not visible from the air. While slow; there was just enough energy remaining to cause the aircraft to flip onto its back. There were no injuries and the damage is minimal. One action that may have prevented the incident would be to mount larger tires to the aircraft for planned off airport landings. Another would be to cut all vegetation from intended landing area on owned land.'
rationale: The pilot conducted multiple low reconnaissance passes over the intended landing area and selected a location with no visible tall weeds, but dense undergrowth not detectable from the air caused the aircraft to flip during rollout. The pilot identified that larger tundra tires or pre-cleared vegetation would have mitigated the risk. The decision to land on an unimproved surface where complete hazard identification was not achievable from the air reflects a situational awareness and aeronautical decision-making limitation—the procedures for off-airport operations were understood, but the human operator could not fully assess the terrain hazard from the available vantage point.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Sick Passenger which required us to call STAT-MD through ARINC. Using the VHF frequency chart on one of the last pages of our [release]; we attempted to call ARINC; which we could talk to; but barely understood them. We tried a different frequency which was no better. We needed to talk to Dispatch somehow but were unable to through ARINC and our ACARS showed ACARS NO COMM. While we finally patched with STAT-MD and Dispatch; we wasted around five minutes; which during those minutes we were doing nothing to get ahead of this sick Passenger's situation. ACARS and ARINC is not always reliable.'
rationale: During a sick-passenger emergency requiring urgent contact with STAT-MD and Dispatch, the crew's ACARS showed 'NO COMM' and the published VHF frequencies for ARINC were barely intelligible, consuming approximately five minutes during which no medical coordination progress was made. The communications infrastructure—ACARS and the published ARINC frequencies—failed to provide the reliable connectivity the procedure assumed would be available for in-flight medical emergencies. Because correctly-following crews are left without an effective communication path when these systems degrade, this is a procedure error rooted in the absence of a robust, redundant emergency communication protocol for medical diversion scenarios.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Received a box of mail from the Post Office tagged for ZZZ agent scanning the mail did not pay attention to what was being scanned. Looking at the side of the box clearly shows corrosive marking on the package. The mail was scanned to flight and loaded; ZZZ caught the error and removed package and returned to the Post Office.'
rationale: A mail handler scanned a package bearing clearly visible corrosive markings onto a flight without recognizing or acting on the DG labeling—an error that was caught by the downstream station, not by any pre-loading verification step at the origin. The corrosive marking was visible on the side of the box and unambiguous; the root cause is the individual mail scanner's failure to perform the basic visual DG check that the acceptance procedure requires. This is a human factors error of inattention and non-compliance with the required inspection task by the ground handling personnel.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Around XAPM a student and I were on a lesson doing touch and go's in the pattern at ZZZ. There was a Civil Air Patrol aircraft that was on its way in. ZZZ Tower told the CAP plane (C127) to report a 3 mile final for runway XX. Our aircraft was on an extended downwind looking for the traffic. The C172 had reported a 2.7 mile final for RWY XX and the tower nor my aircraft was sure where the C172 was. The C172 reported us in sight and Tower asked if we had them in sight. I spotted them withing 400' above us crossing from left to right on a 45 degree final approach leg while we were on a 4.5 mile extended downwind at 1;000' AGL. I took precautionary action and descended down to 800' as the aircraft passed above us. After that we were told to fall in line on final behind the civil air patrol aircraft. Once we landed tower asked both us and the CAP pilot to call him. I spoke with tower controller and he stated that the CAP plane was in the wrong and that they spoke with him as well.'
rationale: A Civil Air Patrol C172 was instructed to report a three-mile final but instead crossed at a 45-degree final approach leg approximately 400 feet above the reporting aircraft on extended downwind at 1,000 feet AGL—a conflict that Tower and the reporting aircraft did not anticipate because the CAP aircraft's position report was imprecise and its flight path deviated significantly from the assigned reporting point. The CAP pilot was subsequently identified by the controller as being in the wrong. This is a human factors incident driven by the conflicting pilot's failure of situational awareness and position reporting accuracy, which placed the aircraft on an unexpected intercept course through occupied airspace.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I observed this error while working LN (08L). This incident happened on LC (08R).Aircraft X was on approach to land runway 08R and slowing. Field visibility was 1/4 SM and ceiling OVC002. Aircraft Y was behind him with an overtake situation. I noticed the overtake and asked the Local controller (LC) if the monitor was taking care of that. The reply was nope they haven't said a word. After IFR separation was lost the final monitor controller called the LC controller and said are you going to break him out? The LC controller said isn't that your job. The final monitor did nothing with the aircraft; and the LC controller turned around and told the FLM (Front Line Manager) what should I do with these two? The FLM did not respond or give any answer so the controller let him land.This is a common problem at this facility where the final monitors do not pay attention to the traffic. They are reactive and not proactive to the aircraft situations. This is also an issue because final doesn't know if they should be breaking out the aircraft and the LC controller wasn't sure if they should break the aircraft out. During this configuration and with the weather like it was; the final monitor has separation responsibility till one mile final. The final monitors need refresher on what their roles and responsibilities are. The tower should also be reminded of our responsibility during this situation via a briefing.'
rationale: With field visibility at one-quarter mile and an overtake developing between two ILS approaches to 08R, the final monitor controller and the local controller each believed the other had separation responsibility, resulting in neither taking action—and when the front line manager was consulted mid-sequence, no guidance was given, allowing the situation to resolve only by chance. The facility had a documented ambiguity in the roles and responsibilities of the final monitor versus local controller for this specific weather configuration, confirmed by the reporter's statement that controllers were unsure of their jurisdiction. The procedure error is the absence of clear, unambiguous role delineation for separation responsibility in low-visibility simultaneous approach configurations, which enabled a diffusion-of-responsibility breakdown.

text: Narrative 1 - '[We] were behind an A330 with approximately 12 miles of in trail separation; based on my recollection from the range rings on TCAS. We were flying the same route [from departure airport] and had departed behind this aircraft. We had at least 5 miles of separation between us and the A330 when we departed. While climbing through FL210 we began to experience a buffet that I initially thought was light chop. Within a few seconds after that; the aircraft had slowly begun to bank right; and the Captain and I both thought that perhaps we were losing aileron control. However; when the aircraft reached approximately 30 degrees of right bank and the autopilot failed to correct the problem; my Captain correctly disengaged the autopilot and attempted to hand fly the aircraft back into a level flight attitude. It was then that we began getting rocked with left and right banks as we apparently encountered wake turbulence from the A330. After regaining control of the aircraft; we started climbing again. Then; at FL230 or so; we began to experience the same symptoms yet again; only more violently. At this point; and after consulting with the Captain; I asked for a reroute around the A330; which was authorized. No further instances of turbulence occurred after this point. The FAA may need to re-examine wake turbulence separation criteria for the enroute environment. 12 miles was simply not enough at this altitude. The CRJ-200 handles turbulence quite well; but had we been in a CRJ-700 or CRJ-900; the encounter could have been even more violent (assuming the larger wingspan of those airframes would have still subjected it to wake turbulence).'
rationale: A CRJ-200 following an A330 at 12 miles of in-trail separation in the enroute environment encountered severe wake turbulence at FL210 and again at FL230, rolling to approximately 30 degrees of bank with autopilot disconnect, indicating that the FAA's prescribed wake turbulence separation standard for the enroute phase was insufficient to provide protection at those altitudes and aircraft weight pairings. The reporter and the FO both requested a reroute after the second encounter, which resolved the hazard. This is a procedure error because the separation criteria that ATC applied were formally compliant yet demonstrably inadequate to protect trailing aircraft from the A330's persistent wake—pointing to a deficiency in the established enroute wake turbulence standard.

text: Narrative 1 - 'We checked on with ZZZ approach and were assigned the visual approach to XXR. We were given vectors for the right downwind and later told to expect the visual to XXL. We were given vectors to join the localizer and advised of corporate aircraft (Aircraft Y) traffic at our 3 o'clock. I had the aircraft in sight; it appeared it had been following us on the same left downwind. We were told to maintain visual separation with the corporate traffic; maintain 160 knots to the bridge; and cleared for the visual to runway XXL. I had the traffic in sight for the entire base and final legs. Our approach speed was around 156knots due to the wind; so we were not going to have to slow down too much after the bridge. We appeared to be gaining on Aircraft Y and we subsequently got a TA. After 20 seconds or so; we received a 'DESCEND; DESCEND' RA at approximately 1;300 ft. I smoothly complied with the RA (the automation was already off). The CA reported the TCAS RA to the tower. At around 700 feet we simultaneously got a 'MONITOR VERTICAL SPEED' RA and a 'Glide slope' EGPWS warning. I arrested the descent; added power and flew just below the red box which resulted in a slight climb. Shortly thereafter; we received the 'CLEAR OF CONFLICT' advisory. During the procedure; I lost visual contact with the aircraft. We executed a normal go around and conducted an uneventful visual approach and landing to XXL after.'
rationale: While maintaining visual separation with corporate traffic on final and complying with a speed restriction to the bridge, the crew gained on the leading aircraft and received a TCAS RA to descend at approximately 1,300 feet AGL, which then conflicted almost simultaneously with an EGPWS glideslope warning at 700 feet—requiring the crew to arrest descent, add power, and execute a go-around. The competing TCAS and EGPWS alerts placed the crew in a direct conflict between two safety systems with opposing commands at a critically low altitude. The human factor is the crew's progressive loss of visual contact with the traffic during the RA maneuver, which required reliance on TCAS guidance in a geometrically constrained environment—a situational awareness breakdown under high workload on visual approach.

text: Narrative 1 - 'In the beginning of the take off roll we had a NO TAKE OFF EICAS Message and we rejected the take off. That light came on due to a rudder trim not aligned.'
rationale: On the takeoff roll, a NO TAKE OFF EICAS message triggered a rejected takeoff because the rudder trim was not aligned to the takeoff position. The rudder trim setting is a checklist item that should have been verified before the takeoff roll began; its misalignment indicates the before-takeoff checklist was either incompletely executed or the trim position was inadvertently displaced after checklist completion. This is a procedure error in the execution of the pre-takeoff verification sequence: the aircraft's own protection system prevented a potentially hazardous departure, indicating the checklist safeguard was not fully effective in ensuring correct configuration before roll.

text: Narrative 1 - 'While in the approach phase of flight on the BLUZZ1 RNAV arrival into MEM; ATC advised us we were 5 miles in trail of a heavy. While descending near CLARK at 8800 ft and 210 KIAS; we encountered moderate/severe wake turbulence associated with the [wide body transport] and a light left quartering headwind. Our aircraft abruptly rolled right approximately 45-60 degrees; autopilot disconnected and stall warbler was activated. I immediately took the controls; regained control of the aircraft; applied full thrust; and initiated a climbing right turn to approximately 10;000 ft. I immediately advised ATC I was recovering from the wake turbulence and in a climbing right turn to avoid further encounters. ATC gave us a turn to offset the course of the [wide body transport] and a descent to remain above the preceding aircraft. Once stable; I called the flight attendant to make sure she and all passengers were unharmed. We remained above the path of the [wide body transport] for the duration of the flight and landed with no further instances. [Suggest] greater distance for heavy in trail aircraft.'
rationale: A CRJ was given five miles of in-trail spacing behind a heavy transport on the BLUZZ1 RNAV arrival, and at 8,800 feet encountered moderate-to-severe wake turbulence that caused a 45–60-degree uncommanded roll, autopilot disconnect, and stall warning activation. Five miles of separation in a structured arrival environment was the ATC-applied standard, yet it was categorically insufficient to protect the trailing aircraft from the heavy's wake at that altitude and configuration. As with other wake turbulence separation incidents, the procedure error lies in the application of a formal separation standard that proved inadequate for the specific aircraft pairing and conditions, not in any failure of the crew's response once the encounter began.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Assigned Line 1 to park at the gate. Upon arrival abeam the gate; was notified by Operations that there was no crew available for at least 15 minutes. Coordinated with Ramp to hold at Spot A. Instructed to transition to Line 2 to hold at Spot A. Crew cleared right/left and started the 180 degree right turn transition from Line 1 to Line 2 slowly. Approximately at 135 degrees of turn; FO detected a rapid moving luggage tug with single cart moving south on the lane between two gates. Vehicle had no intention to yield; requiring crew to stop the aircraft. Early detection allowed for a smooth stop without incident. Vehicle cleared and parked; and crew continued as directed to hold at Spot A.  Flight eventually parked without incident.'
rationale: While executing a 180-degree right turn from one taxi line to another at ramp speed, the FO detected a luggage tug moving rapidly southbound in the lane between gates with no intention to yield, requiring an immediate stop. The baggage vehicle operator did not slow or yield despite the aircraft being in the intersection, creating a ground conflict that required the flight crew to react defensively. This is a human factors incident in the 'Other' category where a non-aviation ground vehicle operator's failure to yield right-of-way to a taxiing aircraft created the hazard—the flight crew detected the conflict early and stopped without incident.

text: Narrative 1 - 'On descent into PAFA talking to FAI approach noticed TCAS traffic in a climb at our 11 o'clock moving to 12 o'clock position approximately 3-5 miles away. We were IMC; in the descent to 7000 ft. No traffic call of any sorts from Approach Control about the traffic. As the traffic closed in on us; I disconnected the autopilot and initiated a climb to avoid the traffic. About the same time; we received a TA followed immediately by a RA. Still no call from Approach Control. My FO (First Officer) called Approach Control to advise of our traffic conflict and that we were climbing to avoid. Multiple alarms were heard in the background of the Approach Controller's transmission when [the Controller] called back to command a climb clearance to us. We passed above the traffic by approximately 400 ft. We then cleared the traffic and descended back to 7000 ft. and landed uneventfully.'
rationale: In IMC on descent into PAFA, a TCAS target was visible climbing at the crew's 11 o'clock for several miles as it closed, yet Approach Control issued no traffic advisory during the entire closure period—making no call even as the crew disconnected the autopilot to climb and the TA and RA activated. When the FO called Approach to report the conflict, multiple alarms were audible in the background of the controller's response, suggesting the controller was task-saturated and had not monitored the converging traffic. The procedure error is the controller's failure to apply the required traffic separation service in IMC: issuing no advisory on a tracked converging target in instrument conditions represents a breakdown in the ATC radar separation responsibility.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Routine intro flight with a client. We finished our hour of pre-flight and began taxing down taxiway alpha with the intent of taking off from Runway XX. As we were taxing I the instructor was trying to teach to the client how we taxi using the rudders and brakes. I wanted the client to understand how it felt to Control the airplane using the rudders on the ground. I thought I was clear that the brakes are independent and need to be applied together equally to stop straight ahead. As we approach Taxiway 1X I asked the client to begin slowing down using the brakes. Without hesitation the client immediately began to apply hard pressure but only on the left brake. The airplane screeches to the left and the left main tire impacts a taxi light. I was able to correct back onto the taxiway but not fast enough to avoid damaging the taxi light. We immediately begin taxing back to the FBO to inspect for damage. After visual inspection I conclude that the aircraft was undamaged. I believe the event might be the result of a miscommunication and understanding of the independent brakes. But even after the incident while we were taxing back the client still was unable to stop the aircraft straight ahead without slightly veering to the left even after I explained it a second time. I would say to prevent this incident from happening in the future. Instructors on intro flights should taxi even slower than usual and always remain alert to the possibility of impulsive actions.'
rationale: During an introductory flight, the instructor allowed a non-pilot client to operate the differential brakes while taxiing toward a taxiway intersection without first establishing that the client understood independent brake operation, and when the client applied hard left-only brake pressure the aircraft veered into a taxi light. The instructor's own analysis identifies the probable miscommunication about independent brake function and notes the client continued to veer left even after a second explanation. The correct taxi procedure was known; the instructor's premature delegation of brake control to an untrained individual without adequate verification of understanding was the human error that led to the ground strike.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I want to report a near miss on Aircraft X today. During pushback the captain advised 'clear to push after Aircraft Y. The crew did not see an Aircraft Y; assumed there was no and started the push. Then he noticed the arriving Aircraft Y and both aircrafts came to stop.'
rationale: The captain issued a clearance to push back 'after Aircraft Y,' but the crew could not see the referenced aircraft and assumed none was present, initiating the pushback before visually confirming the traffic was clear—resulting in a near-collision with the arriving Aircraft Y before both aircraft stopped. The push clearance referenced a conditional traffic element that the pushing crew could not independently verify, and no confirmation step or hold was included to ensure the crew had positive visual identification of Aircraft Y before commencing the push. This is a procedure error in the pushback clearance format: issuing a conditional clearance tied to traffic the crew cannot see, without a mandatory hold-until-sighted confirmation step, created a predictable gap between the intended and actual safety state.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Aircraft X pushed back from gate tail south. Taxi instructions were north on U; U3 cross 4L; H. All four of us on the flight deck concurred with the taxi instructions. As I started my left turn from U to U3 aircraft Y zipped past us northbound on Q. I had to stop quickly. Saw more aircraft northbound on Q. I set parking brake; we told ATC about aircraft Y and queried about our clearance. They reiterated we were cleared U3 cross 4L. Other aircraft were now stopped and we proceeded without incident.'
rationale: All four flight deck crew members concurred on a taxi clearance via U and U3, then while turning from U onto U3, Aircraft Y crossed rapidly northbound on the parallel Q taxiway—an aircraft that was not part of the taxi instruction the crew had accepted and was not expected in that lane. The crew stopped immediately without incident and queried ATC, who confirmed their clearance was correct. This is a human factors incident in the 'Other' category where a third-party aircraft moving unexpectedly in adjacent taxi lanes created a near-conflict; the reporting crew's situational awareness and prompt stop prevented any ground contact.

text: Narrative 1 - 'The flight was a ferry flight with no passengers or flight attendant. It was an empty aircraft with 1500 lbs of ballast fuel added. While holding; we calculated decision fuel by adding reserve 1800; burn to alternate 800; and a burn of about 200 lbs to the destination which gave us a decision fuel about around 2800 lbs. We held till we had 3800 lbs and I decided to divert to the alternate because the weather over our destination was not moving and it was becoming apparent that we were not going to have enough time to get in. Dispatch also advised us that the approximately 800 lbs burn was at 17000 ft altitude (we were at 12;000 ft). When we were enroute to the alternate; Dispatch advised us that we couldn't burn into the 1500 lbs of ballast fuel that we had. So our real reserve was not 1800 lbs but 3300 lbs. Since we were already on our way to the alternate at this point; and the weather was good; I made the decision to continue to the alternate. We landed with a little over 3000 lbs which was below the reserve fuel of 3300 lbs. There was confusion over whether our reserve fuel was 1800 lbs or 3300 lbs and we didn't realize it was the higher number till advised by Dispatch. Dispatch also advised me that their software doesn't account for ballast fuel when planning for decision fuel and as a result we were not advised of the higher number earlier during the flight. I contacted a few other pilots and there is definitely confusion over whether the ballast fuel is used for actual ballast or to 'trick' the weight and balance program to giving us a proper calculation. I will verify the release paperwork for the 'reserve' fuel number and not just assume it to be in the vicinity of 1800 lbs. I think the company should clarify the reason for the ballast fuel and specific requirements of the ballast fuel for empty flights as there seems to be confusion about it. In addition; pilots should be made aware of this potential for confusion. Because of the added stress of holding during weather events; the potential for this to be overlooked is greater.'
rationale: Dispatch's flight planning software did not account for 1,500 pounds of ballast fuel when computing the decision fuel figure provided to the crew, causing the crew to calculate and operate against a reserve of 1,800 pounds when the actual operationally protected reserve was 3,300 pounds—a discrepancy Dispatch disclosed only after the crew had already diverted. The crew landed below the true reserve threshold through no error of their own fuel management; they operated on incorrect figures supplied by a dispatch system with a documented calculation deficiency. This is a procedure error because the dispatch planning tool had a known failure mode—ballast fuel exclusion—that produced a materially wrong reserve number without flagging the discrepancy to the crew.

text: Narrative 1 - 'We briefed the Runway 15 visual approach (appch) into ASE. About 6-7 miles out we got a pull up" single chime alert. The left seat Flying Pilot was already in transition to slow the descent rate when the alert came. Small pitch adjustments were already in progress. We did not elect to do a go around as doing so may have been more problematic as there was no missed app procedure for the visual appch. The hill was plainly in site and was not considered a threat in VFR conditions.We entered the slope rates into the Garmin thinking this would be a much more accurate path for the Garmin to fly. This may have caused a path disparity causing the airplane to slightly pitch over to grab the next slope. NO place does it state in the ASE visual 15 appch NOT to do so. Both crew recently flew this visual appch with no trouble months prior. They were shot without slope inputs entered. Make a note in the appch plate stating "Do not adjust slope rates""'
rationale: While briefing and flying the Runway 15 visual approach into Aspen, the crew entered slope rate inputs into the Garmin—a common customization for precision path management—but the approach plate contained no prohibition against doing so, and this input appears to have caused a path disparity triggering a single-chime terrain pull-up alert. The crew had recently flown the same approach without slope inputs and without incident. The procedure error lies in the approach documentation: a known incompatibility between slope-rate FMS customization and the published visual approach path profile was not captured as a restriction or caution on the approach plate, leaving crews without the guidance needed to avoid inadvertently degrading the path computation.
